title: Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople
body:
The Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople (Greek: Οἰκουμενικὸν Πατριαρχεῖον Κωνσταντινουπόλεως, romanized: Oikoumenikón Patriarkhíon Konstantinoupóleos) is the First-Throne Church among the patriarchates and the autocephalous jurisdictions that compose the Eastern Orthodox Church. As the Mother Church of the modern Eastern Orthodox Patriarchates and Autocephalous Churches, it holds a unique status in Christendom, having historically granted autocephaly to these daughter churches through its canonical authority. It is headed by the Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople, the primus inter pares (first among equals) among all Eastern Orthodox primates. 
Due to its historical pre-eminence as the Church of the capital of the former Eastern Roman Empire, and its foundational role in the dissemination of the Christian faith, the Patriarchate of Constantinople holds the primacy of honor and responsibility within Eastern Orthodox Christianity. As such, the Ecumenical Patriarch is recognized as the primary representative and spiritual leader of the world's Eastern Orthodox Christians. Phanar (Turkish: Fener), the historic neighborhood of Istanbul, where the Patriarchate and the Patriarchal Cathedral of St. George are located, and where the Patriarch resides, is widely used as a metonym or shorthand for the institution itself.
The Ecumenical Patriarchate promotes the expansion of the Christian faith and Christian Orthodox doctrine. The Ecumenical Patriarchs are actively involved in ecumenism and interfaith dialogue, charitable work, while remaining steadfast in the defense of Orthodox Christian traditions. Key priorities and prominent issues for the Ecumenical Patriarchate in the 21st century include the safety of believers in the Middle East, the reconciliation and potential reunion between Eastern Orthodoxy and both the Catholic Church and the Oriental Orthodox Churches, and the reopening of the Theological School of Halki, which was closed down by the Turkish authorities in 1971.
